title: Quiz: Logical Operations
description: Test your understanding of transversal gates and magic state distillation

questions.question: What makes a gate 'transversal'?

options.text: It acts on multiple qubits simultaneously
Operations are applied to corresponding qubits independently - errors cannot spread within a block
It has the shortest circuit depth

questions.explanation: A transversal gate applies the same operation to corresponding qubits of code blocks independently. This ensures that a single fault cannot cause errors on multiple qubits within the same block, preserving fault-tolerance.

questions.question: What does the Eastin-Knill theorem state?

options.text: All gates can be made transversal with the right code
No quantum error-correcting code has a universal set of transversal gates

questions.explanation: The Eastin-Knill theorem proves that no QEC code can implement a universal gate set entirely through transversal operations. This is why we need magic state distillation or other techniques for non-Clifford gates like T.

questions.question: What is the purpose of magic state distillation?

options.text: To purify noisy magic states into high-fidelity states for non-Clifford gates

questions.explanation: Magic state distillation takes multiple noisy magic states and produces fewer, higher-fidelity magic states. These purified states enable fault-tolerant implementation of non-Clifford gates like T through gate teleportation.

questions.question: In the 15-to-1 distillation protocol, how does output error scale with input error p?
questions.explanation: The 15-to-1 protocol produces output error ~ 35*p^3 from input error p. This cubic suppression allows rapid improvement in fidelity with repeated rounds of distillation.

questions.question: The Steane code [[7,1,3]] has a transversal T gate.
questions.explanation: False. The Steane code has transversal H, S, and CNOT gates, but NOT the T gate. The T gate must be implemented via magic state injection, which is a consequence of the Eastin-Knill theorem.

questions.question: Why is T-count important in fault-tolerant quantum computing?

options.text: T gates are the only gates available
T gates are orders of magnitude more expensive than Clifford gates
T gates are the fastest gates
T gates don't require error correction

questions.explanation: T gates require magic state distillation, which consumes many physical qubits and time steps. Clifford gates are transversal and cheap. Minimizing T-count is crucial for practical fault-tolerant algorithms.

questions.question: What is the magic state |T> used for?

options.text: Implementing the T (pi/8) gate via gate teleportation

questions.explanation: |T> = (|0> + e^{i*pi/4}|1>)/sqrt(2) is the magic state used to implement the T gate. The gate is applied via teleportation: controlled-S with the magic state, followed by X measurement and Clifford correction.

questions.question: Which gates are typically transversal in CSS codes like the Steane code? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: CSS codes like Steane typically have transversal H, S (phase), and CNOT. The T gate is NOT transversal in any code that has transversal CNOT (by Eastin-Knill), so it requires magic states.
